summary: Gruber examines the perennial speculation about Apple acquiring Disney, prompted by a Hollywood Reporter piece suggesting such a deal shouldn't be ruled out. He argues that while the cultural fit between the two companies is unusually strong — both are quality-obsessed, family-friendly iconic brands with devoted fan bases — Apple's acquisition strategy has always favored small, quiet purchases rather than industry-reshaping megadeals. He contrasts Apple's approach with Microsoft's history of massive acquisitions and notes that Disney's century-long identity as an independent company makes a sale unlikely. A lengthy footnote explores the tantalizing counterfactual of Apple buying Pixar instead of Disney in 2006, concluding it would have been a perfect cultural and strategic fit but was historically impossible.
key_points: Apple's acquisition strategy centers on small, quiet purchases (averaging one per month), with the $3 billion Beats deal remaining its largest ever — a stark contrast to Microsoft's eight acquisitions exceeding that amount

The Apple-Disney speculation has legs because the cultural overlap is genuinely strong: both companies prioritize quality, design, and engineering, and both have fan bases rather than mere customer bases
If Disney were ever to sell, Apple is essentially the only tech company Disney would plausibly trust to let Disney remain Disney, with Berkshire Hathaway as the only other conceivable candidate

The counterfactual of Apple buying Pixar instead of Disney in 2006 is explored at length — it would have been a perfect fit in the abstract but was impossible given Apple's size, strategic focus, and the optics of Jobs selling his own company to Apple
Berkshire Hathaway's Apple holdings account for 46% of its entire public stock portfolio, creating an indirect connection to any hypothetical Apple-Disney deal
money_quotes: They'd need a longer statement if they bought Disney.

I'm in no way arguing that Apple couldn't afford to buy Disney, even at a generous markup, but rather that enormous industry-shuffling acquisitions like that aren't what Apple does, nor, in my opinion, should do.
Why drink Veuve Clicquot when you can afford Dom Perignon? If you're going to buy, buy the best.
If Disney were to look for a buyer, I can't think of a single big-enough-to-afford-it company other than Apple that they would want to sell to.
Apple today generates more profit every 5 days than it did in the entire year of 2006.
Buying Pixar without acquiring those rights would have been like, well, buying Disney today without acquiring Mickey Mouse, Snow White, Star Wars, and Marvel.
Anyone who doesn't appreciate it as a nearly perfect website is the sort of fool who doesn't recognize that, sometimes, a good slice of buttered toast is just the thing and all you need.
key_insight: The Apple-Disney acquisition fantasy persists because the cultural fit is genuinely exceptional, but Apple's DNA is built around small, quiet acquisitions that integrate seamlessly — not industry-reshaping megadeals that would fundamentally change what Apple is.
